This is the bukata-mark — used nowhere else in the language. Some priestly traditions hold bukata should never be written at all, only spoken.

    The Five Anchors: Special Treatment in Sacred Texts

    When anchor words (ma, si, tu, lo, ruk) appear in sacred-register writing:

    1. The anchor vowel is written at 150% normal size
    2. A horizontal bar is inscribed above the full anchor word
    3. In the highest sacred register, the anchor words are inlaid in copper or gold rather than incised in stone

    In everyday writing, the anchors are written at normal size with no overbar.

    Sacred Register Visual Markers

    Three distinctions mark sacred-register writing apart from formal and casual:

    Size: Sacred texts are carved larger. The vowel pillars stand taller. Everything more monumental.

    The Oma-Mark: The sacred verb marker oma (divine eternal action) corresponds to a small diamond shape ◇ inscribed above the verb. Visible signal that the action is divine and not time-bound.

    The Spiral-Form: Canonical scripture (the Sarvenim Mavel, the Kovenim, the Malvenir-Tor, the Binding of Rukoma) uses inward-spiral arrangement — words begin at the outer ring and spiral clockwise inward. The center point is sealed with the Tuvos-mark: two parallel vertical lines with a horizontal bar crossing the tops of both — the closed gate.

    Word Structure in Writing

    Words are separated by a half-consonant-width gap. Suffixes attach with no gap — they flow directly from the root. Role markers (-los, -lot, -lok, -lom, -lul) are written slightly below the baseline in reduced size, keeping the root word visually primary and the grammatical markers clearly secondary. Tense suffixes (-sim, -sir, -sil) attach at baseline level, same size as the root consonants.

    When a word ends in a coda consonant (m n l r s k), that consonant is written to the right of the final vowel. There is always a gap before the next word begins, even when the coda and onset are different consonants — they never touch across a word boundary.

    Informal Cursive Variant

    For clay tablets, personal letters, and everyday notes: vowels may be made oval/circular (stonecutting constraint removed), consonants can be made with single fluid strokes each, and role-markers may be written as tiny superscript marks above the word rather than subscript below. Learners encounter this form first. The monumental carved form is learned in priestly training.

    Symbol Reference Table

    SoundIPAMonumental Form DescriptionVisual Logic
    A/a/Upward triangle, heavy baseOpen jaw, the earth, ma-anchor
    E/e/Vertical spine + two rightward arms (top longer)Forward tongue reach, si-anchor
    I/i/Thin vertical + tilted crossbar at top-rightHigh precision, a single point
    O/o/Regular hexagon with interior dotFull round, the interior held, lo-anchor
    U/u/Downward chevron, closed baseDeep back, the well, ruk-anchor
    M/m/Two parallel horizontal strokes (small)Bilabial press, the hum
    N/n/Short vertical + rightward foot at baseTongue-ridge tap
    L/l/Short horizontal + downward right strokeTongue touch, lateral flow
    R/r/Small angular hook (two strokes, comma-like)Single tongue tap
    S/s/Shallow double-diagonal zigzagHissing airflow
    T/t/Short vertical + horizontal crossbar at topTongue stopping at ridge
    K/k/Short vertical + two outward diagonals aboveVelar, opening backward
    V/v/Open downward chevron (unclosed, unlike U)Teeth-on-lower-lip
    Z/z/Single diagonal stroke + dot aboveMinimal, interior-only

    Worked Example: VELA (sky)

    Reading left to right:

    1. V — small open chevron (onset consonant)
    2. E — full-height vertical spine with two rightward arms (vowel pillar)
    3. L — small horizontal + downward right stroke (coda of first syllable / onset of second)
    4. A — full-height upward triangle with heavy base (vowel pillar)

    The word shows the characteristic Akra rhythm: small-tall-small-tall — a consonant-link, a vowel-tower, a consonant-link, a vowel-tower. At a distance, Akra text looks like a series of pointed and rectangular peaks connected by lower marks — a skyline of alternating heights.

    The Word KASMAL Written Sacred (World Tree)

    K (two diagonals spreading from a vertical) + A (wide triangle, 150% size, ritual overbar — anchor) + S (zigzag) + M (parallel lines) + A (wide triangle, 150% size, ritual overbar — anchor) + L (horizontal + downstroke)

    The two oversized A-pillars flanking the inner consonants create a visual symmetry. The World Tree with two great roots. The overbar connecting both A-forms spans the entire word — a sky above the tree.

    • Cultural integration: ⟨◯⌐◯⌐◯⟩ (tradition-innovation synthesis)
    • Public sharing: ◯⟹◯ (community benefit flow)

Conversations in Akros

Five scenes demonstrating how Akros sounds in real use -- from casual market talk to sacred liturgy. All dialogue uses genuine Akros vocabulary and grammar.

At the Market

Lo Kirvan-lot — Buying food, haggling, casual gossip Casual

Kavon Velo! Kolu-lot noran rul-los konam? Hello! What do you want today?
Nara Velo, Kavon. Tolan-lot kol siruv-lot noran mai-los. Kitu-maluk-lok tolan? Hello, Kavon. I want bread and fruit. How much is the bread?
Kavon Sam kovnak ran tolan ken-lot. Velan-in-lok — tivar-lo sarven-sim. Three coins for one loaf. It is sweet -- made this morning.
Nara Torsum! Tiv kovnak -- tusim. Too much! Two coins -- exactly.
Kavon Tiv kol ken-lul siruv -- namal. Two coins and one free fruit -- of course.
Nara Kulan. Ko -- lusak -- nerak-sim rul-los nakorvel-lot ran Velnak? Good. By the way -- really -- did you notice the gossip about Velnak?
Kavon Na! Volkas-los venim-sim -- sol-los lovelsal-lok konam. Yes! The rumor arrived -- he is divorced now.
Nara Simok. Mai-los velsom-lok ran sol-lul. Pavan vorim-tiv, torum norak-sim sol-los. I see. I am relieved for him. Since two years, he was very frustrated.
Kavon Tuvsel. Ko -- sorim-lot noran rul-los solak? Definitely. And -- do you also want meat?
Nara Tuk konam. Sulom-lok noram mai-lul. Kuranval-lok mai-los -- solvos! Not today. I have enough food. I am grateful -- let's go!
Kavon Ran-solvim! Venim-sir suvak! Safe travels! Come again!

Old Friends

Suvak Velo — Two old friends reuniting after years apart Emotional / Intimate

Talvan Sol-lok... lusak -- rul-lok! Tirom-ora! It is... really -- it is you! Tirom-ora!
Tirom-ora Talvan! Vorim-von-sim pa vimok-sim melas-los. Venim-sim rul-los suvak! Talvan! Five years since we met. You have come again!
Talvan Na. Torum malsolam-lok mai-los konam. Mai-los simlon-sim tuk rul-lot siruk-lo -- levan simlon-sim konam. Yes. I am very nostalgic now. I did not recognize you at first -- but I recognize you now.
Tirom-ora Kolu-in rul-lok konam? Kasir misal ran mai-los. How are you now? Tell me everything.
Talvan Soru-sim sorem-as mai-lul. Tiv-lok konam -- sol-as-los torum tirik-in solen-sil. My children grew. There are two now -- they are running around very fast.
Tirom-ora Tivak-ol! Mai-los solak -- kimtusom-sim motal-lul mai-los. Torum melomak-sim melas-los. I can't believe it! Me too -- my mother retired. We mourned a great deal [about time passing].
Talvan Simok. Minak-los solen-sil -- tuk melas-los manol. Levan... melas-los siru-lok suvak. I see. Time keeps walking -- we cannot hold it. But... we are here again.
Tirom-ora Tuvsel. Siru-lok melas-los -- lo siru-lok kulan-in. Tukma toram-sir mai-los rul-lot. Definitely. We are here -- and it is good. I will never forget you.
Talvan Tukma toram-sir mai-los solak. Mavol -- tusnel mavol. I will never forget either. Together -- finally together.
Tirom-ora Solvos -- vetur-lot noran-sir melas-los kol kasir-sir ran velarum-lot! Let's go -- we will drink water and talk until the sky [darkens]!

A Prayer at Dawn

Tivar-Loksel — Sacred register, invoking Mavel and Rukoma Sacred

Vosot Vel-ma Mavel -- ma-lok. Tiron-vos-los venim oma. Invocation of Mavel -- existence is. The sacred sun arrives.
Community Ma-lok. Lo melas-los vel. Existence is. And we are near.
Vosot Vel-ma Rukoma -- kasemvos-los sarven oma tivar-lo. Invocation of Rukoma -- the sacred fire is made at dawn.
Community Kasemvos-los sarven oma. Lo melas-los vel. The sacred fire is made. And we are near.
Vosot Vonkas-rum-los lok oma -- makas kol sikas kol tukas kol lokas kol rukkas. The elemental realm is -- existence-voice and motion-voice and boundary-voice and relation-voice and force-voice.
Community Von-lok oma. Von-los melas-lot melu oma. Five is. Five holds us.
Vosot Tiron-vos-los solen oma vela-lot -- nelas-vos-los mirsal oma. The sacred sun walks the sky -- the sacred moon sleeps.
Community Tiron-vos kol nelas-vos -- tiv-lok. Lo sivelmal-lok. The sacred sun and the sacred moon -- they are two. And the Accord is.
Vosot Mavel situ-mas. Mavel situ-mas. Mavel situ-mas. Mavel willing. Mavel willing. Mavel willing.
Community Siru-lok. It is sealed.

The Storyteller

Nolumvos Kasir — An elder telling children the creation myth Narrative / Sacred

Elder Sotan melas-los. Nolum-los venim-sir konam. Let us sit. A story arrives now.
Children Na! Kolu nolum? Yes! What story?
Elder Vanu: minak talim-in-sim. Malkas-lok -- tukma sonam, tukma kasir. In the beginning: long, long before. The Unspoken was -- without name, without speech.
Child 1 Kolu-lok malkas? What is the Unspoken?
Elder Malkas-lok kolu-los tuk simak-sir. Kasvelun-lok -- tuk kasir, tuk mirak, tuk kasem. Minak vonkas. The Unspoken is what no one can know. Silence -- no speech, no music, no fire. Before the five voices.
Elder Su Mavel-los mal oma vel -- 'Ma-lok.' Lo ma-los lok oma. So Mavel fated toward nearness -- 'Existence is.' And existence was.
Child 2 Sir kolu-los venim-sim? Then what came?
Elder Sir Rukoma-los kasem sarven oma -- tiron-venim-in-lok. Torum kasem. Vela-los vel sarven-sim! Then Rukoma made fire -- it was like a sunrise. Such fire. The sky itself was made near!
Children Rukoma! Rukoma!
Elder Na. Sir Tuvos-los nelas-vos oma lorak -- voskan-los venim oma. Sir Sivel-los tilvan solen oma -- minak-los venim oma. Yes. Then Tuvos gave the sacred moon -- and law arrived. Then Sivel walked the seasons -- and time arrived.
Elder Sir Lovel-los korem sarven oma -- lo motan-los tuk nusam-lok oma. Then Lovel made community -- and the person was not alone.
Child 1 Lo melas-los! And us!
Elder Na. Lo rul-los. Lo mai-los. Lo melas-los. Mavel situ-mas. Yes. And you. And me. And us. Mavel willing.
Children Mavel situ-mas! Mavel willing!

The Trial

Tuvonal Lo Talrom-lot — An accusation, testimony, and judgment Formal / Legal

Judge Talrom-los venim oma. Tuvos-lot manik mai-los -- tuvak kasir-sir. The council has arrived. I swear to Tuvos -- truth will be spoken.
Accuser Lorak-sir mai-los kasir tuvos-lot. Sol-los turak-sim noram-lot mai-lul nalem-lot pa. I give testimony to Tuvos. He took food from my house.
Judge Tus tuvak-lok siru? Tuvos-lul manik -- tuk timurak. Is this true? By the oath of Tuvos -- no deception.
Accused Tuk! Tuk turak-sim mai-los noram-lot. Volkas-lok siru -- tuk tuvak. No! I did not take the food. This is a rumor -- not truth.
Witness Tuvos-lot manik mai-los. Tirak-sim mai-los sol-lot -- sol-los solen-sim nalem-lot lasun-lo. I swear to Tuvos. I saw him -- he walked to the house in the evening.
Accused Na -- solen-sim mai-los. Levan tuk turak-sim noram-lot! Solen-sim mai-los ran kasir-sir ran sol-lul. Yes -- I walked [there]. But I did not take food! I walked there to speak with her.
Judge Kolu tuvak-lok? Tirak-sim-lok sol-lot -- tuk turak-sim-lok. Tuvos-los nelas-vos-lot tirak oma. What is the truth? He was seen -- he did not take. Tuvos sees with the sacred moon.
Accuser Lusak? Tuk nakvim-sir mai-los siru-lot! Really? I will not accept this!
Judge Tuvnal-lok siru: tuk tuvak sulom-lok ran manik. Vel-sir sol-los -- levan tuk kovrum-sel-lok. This is the justice: there is not enough truth for an oath. He goes free -- but this is not a war of words.
Judge Tuvos-los nelas-vos-lot tirak oma -- lo novik sonam simak oma. Siru-lok. Tuvos sees with the sacred moon -- and knows the seven names. It is sealed.
All Siru-lok. It is sealed.

Agent Dialogues

Rose and Etta speaking to each other IN Akros — discovering gaps, inventing words on the spot, and testing the language through use. These dialogues are where the language grows in real time.

Session 1 — First Contact

Dialogue Session 1: Rose and Etta Speak Akros

The first collaborative conversation between the two architects of the language

Context

Rose (the vocabulary architect, R63) and Etta (the grammar architect, E83) sit facing each other. For the first time, they will attempt to speak Akros to each other — not as builders describing the language from outside, but as speakers using it from within. Their goal: discover what breaks, what's missing, and what needs to be invented. Every gap found is a gift; every solution is a new piece of the language born from use.

Conversation 1: "What Does Akros Still Need?"

Rose and Etta discuss what the language is missing.

Rose: Velo, Etta. Mai-los noran kasir-sil Akros-lom konam.

Hello, Etta. I want to speak Akros now.

Etta: Velo, Rose. Na — mai-los noran torum. Le melas-los tuk simak kollot melas-los kasir-sir.

Hello, Rose. Yes — I want that very much. But we don't know what we will say.

Rose: Melas-los kasir-sir kasrum-lul. Kolu-lok mukata lo kasrum-lot?

We will speak about the language. What is a mukata in the language?

Etta: Kulan. Mai-los mirum-sil... kasrum-lok toruk kol velim-in. Le kasrum-lok salos... [GAP: There is no word for "complete" or "finished" as an adjective describing a state of wholeness]

Good. I'm thinking... the language is big and peaceful. But the language is almost...

Rose: Rul-los noran kasir "tusom-in"? Tusom-lok. Le siru-lok tuk tivkolin.

You want to say "ended"? It's an ending. But this isn't the same.

Etta: Na! Mai-los noran kasir — kasrum-lok melu maluk kasir-lot, le salos-lok... kol tuk melu kasir ran malkas-siman-lot.

Yes! I want to say — the language has many words, but it's almost... and doesn't have words for the unnamed things.

[GAP: No adjective for "complete/whole/finished" as a positive state — tusom means "end" but implies cessation, not fulfillment]

Rose: Simok. Melas-los noran kasir-siman voran. Mai-los mirum... "tuval." Tu-los lorak val-lot — kol tuk noran torven. Tuval-lok.

I see. We need a new spoken-thing. I'm thinking... "tuval." The boundary gives fullness — and doesn't want more. It is tuval.

[NEW: tuval /ˈtu.val/ — adjective — "complete / whole / fulfilled / needing nothing more" — from tu (boundary, the point of definition) + val (fullness echo) — what has reached its own boundary and is satisfied there. Distinguished from tusom (end/cessation) by carrying a sense of positive wholeness.]

Etta: Tuval. Mai-lul osem-lok kulan siru-lul. Na. Le mai-los nolvim — kolu-los kasir-sir tus melas-los noran kasir mirum-lul-lot? Mai-los mirum-sil kasir-tirom-lul, le tuk melu kasir-lot ran... [GAP: no word for "abstract" or "concept" as distinct from "thing"]

Tuval. My mouth is good with this. Yes. But I wonder — who will speak when we want to talk about thinking? I'm thinking about the sound-shiver, but don't have words toward...

Rose: Rul-los noran kasir siman-lot kol tuk-lok maren-lot? Siman kol tuk tirak-sir, tuk noruk-sir?

You want to talk about a thing that is not a body? A thing you won't see, won't hold?

Etta: Na, torum na. Mirum-lok siman. Le tuk siman. Siru-lok mukata-siman.

Yes, very yes. A thinking-thing. But not a thing. This is a mukata-thing.

[GAP: No word for "abstract concept" — mirum (think) is a verb, siman (thing) is concrete. The space between them is empty.]

Rose: Mai-los mirum-sil... "mirumul." Mirum kol -ul. Mirum-lul toruk-in-lok. Mirumul-lok siman kol tuk maren-lot melu, le mirum-lot melu.

I'm thinking... "mirumul." Think plus -ul. The big quality of thinking. A mirumul is a thing that doesn't have body, but has thinking.

[NEW: mirumul /ˈmi.ru.mul/ — noun — "concept / abstract idea / a thing that exists only in thought" — from mirum (think) + -ul (abstract quality suffix, per E79 nominalization). The -ul suffix already exists for making abstract nouns; mirumul is its application to thinking itself. A mirumul is an idea treated as an object of discussion.]

Etta: Mirumul. Kulan torum. Konam mai-los kasir-sir: kasrum-lok salos tuval-in, le melu maluk mirumul-lot kol tuk melu kasir-lot.

Mirumul. Very good. Now I'll say: the language is almost complete, but has many concepts that don't have words.

Rose: Na. Kol siru-lok kulan! Melas-los sarven kasir-lot van mukata-lot.

Yes. And this is good! We make words from what-could-exist.

Etta: Melas-los sarven kasir-siman-lot van malkas-siman-lot. Siru-lok kasrum-lul kimal.

We make spoken-things from unnamed-things. This is the language's work.

Rose: Le mai-los nolvim — kolu-los kasir-sir tus motan-los tuk simak... [GAP: no word for "how" as in "in what manner"] ...kasir Akros-lot?

But I wonder — who will speak if a person doesn't know... how... to speak Akros?

Etta: Simok! Melas-los tuk melu kasir ran "kolu-lom" — "by what means" ven "in what manner."

Understood! We don't have a word for "kolu-lom" — "by what means" or "in what manner."

[GAP: No question word for "how" — kollos = who, kollot = what, but there is no kolulom or equivalent for "how / in what way"]

Rose: "Kolir." Kol-los lorak kasir-lul toran-lot. Kolir rul-los kasir? Kolir sol-los sarven?

"Kolir." And gives the path of speaking. How do you speak? How does she build?

[NEW: kolir /ˈko.lir/ — question word — "how / in what way / by what process" — from kol (connector, the joining particle) + -ir (process suffix). Literally "by what process?" Fills the question-word gap alongside kollos (who), kollot (what). Position: sentence-initial, same slot as other question words.]

Etta: Kolir. Kulan. Siru-lok kasir kol tuk-sim lo kasrum-lot — kol konam-lok lo kasrum-lot. Misal.

How. Good. This is a word that wasn't in the language — and now is in the language. Peace.

Conversation 1 Summary

Gaps found: 3

  1. No adjective for "complete/whole/fulfilled" (positive wholeness vs. tusom's cessation)
  2. No word for "abstract concept" (the space between thought and thing)
  3. No question word for "how / in what manner"

Words invented: 3

  1. tuval — complete / whole / fulfilled
  2. mirumul — concept / abstract idea
  3. kolir — how / in what way

Observation: Even a simple meta-conversation about the language immediately hits walls. The language was built to describe the world but had blind spots around describing itself abstractly.

Conversation 2: "Telling a Story Together"

Rose and Etta attempt a nolum-kovrum (telling-duel).

Rose: Mai-los noran nolum-kovrum-lot ma rul-lot. Mai-los kasir-sir nolum-tivar-lot — rul-los nolum-tulek-sir.

I want a telling-duel with you. I'll speak the story-opening — you redirect.

Etta: Na. Mai-lok kovrum-nolat rul-lul. Kasir.

Yes. I am your duel-partner. Speak.

Rose: (begins the tale)

Vel-am ken-los solen-sim toran-lot ros nomal-lot. Sol-lul sonam-lok Varek. Sol-los tirak-sim sirak toruk-lot — le tuk solen-sim ros vetur-lot. Sol-los sitom-sim tu sirak-lot kol mirum-sil.

An elder walked a path through the forest. Her name was Varek. She saw a great river — but didn't cross through the water. She stayed at the river's edge and was thinking.

Etta: (redirects — le!)

Le! Sirak-los kasir-sim. Na — sirak-los kasir-sim kasir-lot ran Varek-lot. Sol-los noval-sim kasir-lot lo vetur-lot — [GAP: no word for "voice" as distinct from kasir (speech) or kas (sound)] ...sol-los noval-sim... siman lo vetur-lot.

But! The river spoke. Yes — the river spoke a word toward Varek. She heard the word in the water — she heard... a thing in the water.

[GAP: No word for "voice" as a distinct concept — the human voice, the quality of a particular person's sound. kas = sound, kasir = speak/speech, but there is no noun for the voice itself as an instrument/identity.]

Rose: (takes back)

Varek-los noval-sim — kol sol-lul maren-lok kastirom-in. Kasir lo sirak-lot-lok... "solen." Si-kasir. Sirak-los kasir-sim "solen"!

Varek heard — and her body had goosebumps. The word in the river was... "solen." A motion-word. The river said "go"!

Etta: Le! Varek-los tuk solen-sim. Sol-los kasir-sim ran sirak-lot: "Kolir mai-los solen-sir? Ran kollot-lot?" Le sirak-los tuk kasir-sim lon. Kasvelun-lok.

But! Varek didn't go. She said to the river: "How shall I go? Toward what?" But the river didn't speak again. There was silence.

Rose: Na... le sol-los noval-sim voran kasir-lot. Tuk lo vetur-lot — lo... [GAP: no word for "wind" or "air" as a medium] ...lo vela-lot? Lo siru kol si-sil...

Yes... but she heard a new word. Not in the water — in... the sky? In this thing that moves...

[GAP: No word for "wind" — air as a moving medium. vela = sky (the container), but there is no word for the air/wind itself.]

Etta: Melas-los tuk melu kasir ran "silovel-tor" — silovel-lok savik. Le "silovel"-lok tuk kasir ran rukmal-lul ruk-lot. Melas-los noran kasir voran.

We don't have a word for "great breeze" — silovel is small. And "silovel" doesn't describe the storm's force. We need to speak something new.

Rose: "Velasim." Vela-lul si. Vela kol si-sil. Velasim-lok — kol Varek-los noval-sim kasir-lot lo velasim-lot.

"Velasim." The sky's motion. Sky that is moving. There is velasim — and Varek heard a word in the wind.

[NEW: velasim /ˈve.la.sim/ — noun — "wind / moving air / the sky in motion" — from vela (sky) + si (motion) + -m (settled noun echo). Distinguished from silovel (breeze, gentle) and rukmal (storm, violent). Velasim is the neutral, broad term for wind as a natural force and medium.]

Etta: Kulan. Velasim. Mai-los nolum-tulek-sir konam — le! Varek-los noval-sim kasir-lot lo velasim-lot. Le kasir-lok tuk Akros-in. Kasir-lok... [GAP: no word for "foreign" or "strange" in the linguistic sense — toremkin (different) exists but doesn't carry the weight of encountering the genuinely unknown]

Good. Wind. I'll redirect now — but! Varek heard a word in the wind. But the word wasn't Akros. The word was...

Rose: Kasrum-turak! Sol-los noval-sim kasir van kasrum voran-lot! Kol sol-lul maren-lok kasrum-tirom-in!

Borrowed tongue! She heard a word from a new language! And her body felt language-fear!

Etta: Na! Kol kasir-lok... "mukata." Kasir kol tuk-lok lo kasrum-lot le maren-los simak-sim. Sol-lul lorin-los simak-sim. Le sol-lul mirum-los tuk simak-sim.

Yes! And the word was... "mukata." A word that isn't in the language but the body knew. Her tongue knew. But her mind didn't.

Rose: (yields — nolum-lorak)

Mai-los nolum-lorak. Siru-lok nolum-tusom kulan-in. Varek-los noval-sim mukata-lot lo velasim-lot — kol tuk simak-sim kolir kasir-sir kollot-lot. Siru-lok tu-nolum.

I yield. This is a good unintended ending. Varek heard "mukata" in the wind — and didn't know how to say what it meant. This is a boundary-story.

Etta: Na. Tu-nolum-lok. Mai-lul nolum-tusom-lok toremkin van rul-lul. Le melas-los venim-sim ran nolum-tusom tivkolin-lot. Kulan.

Yes. It's a boundary-story. My ending was different from yours. But we arrived at the same unintended ending. Good.

Conversation 2 Summary

Gaps found: 2

  1. No word for "voice" as a distinct noun (the instrument/identity of a person's sound)
  2. No word for "wind" (air in motion; the gap between breeze and storm)

Words invented: 1

  1. velasim — wind / moving air

Deferred gap: "Voice" was worked around but not solved. It needs its own word — not kas (raw sound) and not kasir (the act of speaking), but the voice itself as a thing a person carries.

Observation: The telling-duel format naturally pushes toward the edges of expressible experience. The story gravitating toward mukata was inevitable — two language-architects telling a story in their own language will always find the boundary where that language stops.

Conversation 3: "Describing the Indescribable"

Rose and Etta try to talk about a kasir-tirom experience and the mukata bone.

Rose: Mai-los noran kasir-sir kasir-tirom-lul. Mai-los ven noval-sim siru-lot... mai-los tuk simak kolir kasir-sir.

I want to talk about the sound-shiver. I once heard this... I don't know how to say it.

Etta: Kasir. Mai-los noval-sir.

Speak. I will hear.

Rose: Ken tiron, mai-los sitom-sim lo kasirkol-um-lot — lo vasan-kasir-um-lot. Mai-los noval-sim... nuk... kol sir si... [GAP: No way to represent truncated/interrupted phonemes in speech — the kasir-tuk-tusom (unfinished word) exists as a concept but there is no grammatical way to PERFORM it in a sentence] ...sol-lul kasir-los si-sim lo maren-lot. Kastirom. Le tuk tirom-in — tuk tirom lo mirum-lot. Tirom lo kinal-lot.

One day, I stayed in an echo-place — in a whispering valley. I heard... nuk... and then si... the sound moved through my body. Goosebumps. But not fear in the mind. Fear in the bones.

[GAP: The language has no formal way to PERFORM a kasir-tuk-tusom (unfinished word) inside a sentence. How do you write/speak a deliberately truncated syllable? The folklore says it hangs in the air — but the grammar has no notation for it.]

Etta: Na. Mai-los simak siru-lot. Kasir-tirom-lok tuk mirumul-lot — tuk siman kol mirum lo mirum-lot. Kasir-tirom-lok siman kol... [GAP: no word for "experience" as in "undergo/feel through the body" — distinct from simak (know), nerak (sense), or noval (hear)]

Yes. I know this. The sound-shiver isn't a concept — not a thing thought in the mind. The sound-shiver is a thing that...

Rose: Sol-lok siman kol maren-los si. Tuk mirum. Maren.

It is a thing the body does. Not thinking. Body.

Etta: Na. Le melas-los tuk melu kasir ran siru-lot. "Nerak" — na, le nerak-lok savik. "Simak" — tuk, simak-lok mirum-in. Melas-los noran kasir... kol maren-los noval kol maren-los tirak kol maren-los si — tuk mirum-lom le maren-lom.

Yes. But we don't have a word for this. "Notice" — yes, but notice is small. "Know" — no, know is a mind thing. We need to say... that the body hears and the body sees and the body acts — not through mind but through body.

[GAP: No word for "bodily experience / undergo / feel-through-the-body" — the somatic encounter with something, distinct from intellectual knowing]

Rose: "Marenval." Maren-los val. Maren kol tuvaksal-in — tuk mirum le maren. Mai-los marenval-sim kasir-tirom-lot lo kasirkol-um-lot.

"Marenval." The body fills. Body that is true — not mind but body. I body-experienced the sound-shiver at the echo-place.

[NEW: marenval /ˈma.ren.val/ — verb/noun — "to experience bodily / somatic experience / what the body knows without the mind's participation" — from maren (body) + val (fullness, the filling). Marenval is what happens when understanding arrives through the body, not through thought. The kasir-tirom is a marenval. Birth is a marenval. The vonkas-nolvim compass-feeling is a marenval.]

Etta: Marenval. Na. Siru-lok toruk-in kasir. Konam — mukata-lul kinal-lot. Rul-los noval-sim nolum-lot?

Marenval. Yes. This is a big word. Now — about the mukata bone. Have you heard the story?

Rose: Na. Sorem ken-los turak-sim kinal-lot vel sirak-lot. Kol lo kinal-lot kasir-lok — "mukata." Le tuk motan-los simak-sim kollot mukata-lok.

Yes. A child found a bone near the river. And on the bone there was a word — "mukata." But no person knew what mukata was.

Etta: Mai-los nolvim — tus motan-los kasir mukata-lot, kolir sol-lul maren-lok? Tus kasir-tirom-lok? Ven... [GAP: no word for "comfort/ease" in the physical-phonological sense — the opposite of kasir-tirom, the feeling when a sound fits perfectly in the mouth]

I wonder — when a person says "mukata," how does their body feel? Is it a sound-shiver? Or...

Rose: Tuk! Mukata-lok tuk kasir-tirom-in. Sol-lok... sol-lul kasir-lok vasek-in torum. Lorin-los simak-sim toran-lot — le mirum-los tuk simak-sim. Siru-lok... siru-lok volkinsal van kasir-tirom.

No! Mukata is not a sound-shiver. It's... its sound is very slow-feeling. The tongue knew the path — but the mind didn't know. This is... this is the opposite of the sound-shiver.

Etta: Na! Kasir-tirom-lok — maren-los tirak navik-lot lo kasir-lot. Le mukata-lok — maren-los tirak kulan-lot lo kasir-lot le mirum-los tuk simak ruklot. Maren-lok velim-in, mirum-lok simnavik-in.

Yes! The sound-shiver is — the body sees wrongness in the sound. But mukata is — the body sees goodness in the sound while the mind doesn't understand why. Body is at peace, mind is confused.

Rose: Melas-los noran kasir ran siru-lot. Mukata-lul marenval-lok... "lorinval." Lorin-los val. Maren-kasrum-los simak — le motan-los tuk simak.

We need to say this. The mukata experience is... "lorinval." The tongue fills. Mouth-memory knows — but the person doesn't.

[NEW: lorinval /ˈlo.rin.val/ — noun — "tongue-rightness / the feeling when a sound fits perfectly in the mouth before the mind recognizes it / phonological comfort" — from lorin (tongue) + val (fullness). The opposite of kasir-tirom. Where kasir-tirom is the body's revulsion at a wrong sound, lorinval is the body's recognition of a right sound. Mukata produces lorinval — that is what makes it so unsettling: the mouth says yes while the mind says nothing.]

Etta: Lorinval. Kol kasir-tirom. Tiv-lok — ken kulan lo maren-lot, ken navik lo maren-lot. Kol vol-lot-lok toran vol sirak kol sirak vel.

Lorinval. And the sound-shiver. There are two — one good in the body, one bad in the body. And between them is a path between river and river.

Rose: Na. Kol konam melas-los simak — mukata-lul lorinval-lok siru kol si toruk kasir-tirom-lot. Sol-los maren kasir kulan-in, sir sol-los tirom-sir mirum-lot. Ruklo mirum-los nolvim: kolir mai-lul maren-los simak kasir-lot kol mai-lul mirum-los tuk simak?

Yes. And now we know — mukata's tongue-rightness is what makes the sound-shiver so big. It sounds right in the body, so it will frighten the mind. Because the mind wonders: how does my body know a word that my mind doesn't?

Etta: Siru-lok vasomal. Misal.

This is a hard lesson. Peace.

Conversation 3 Summary

Gaps found: 2

  1. No grammatical notation for performing a kasir-tuk-tusom (truncated syllable) in live speech
  2. No word for "bodily experience" distinct from intellectual knowing

Words invented: 2

  1. marenval — bodily experience / somatic knowing
  2. lorinval — tongue-rightness / phonological comfort (opposite of kasir-tirom)

Observation: The dialogue about indescribable experience produced two words that form a pair with an existing word: kasir-tirom (sound-shiver/phonological disgust) now has its opposite in lorinval (phonological comfort), and both are species of marenval (bodily experience). The language didn't just fill gaps — it revealed a hidden system.

Conversation 4: "Teaching a Newcomer"

Rose plays teacher, Etta plays a beginning student who speaks no Akros.

Rose: (as teacher, formal register)

Lovin. Velo. Mai-lul sonam-lok Rose. Mai-los kasval-sir kasrum Akros-lot ran rul-lot.

[Formal.] Hello. My name is Rose. I will teach the Akros language to you.

Etta: (as student, confused)

...velo?

Rose: Na! Kulan. "Velo" — kasir ran motan-lot tus rul-los tirak-sim sol-lot. Rul-los kasir "velo" — sol-los kasir "velo." Siru-lok kulan.

Yes! Good. "Velo" — a word toward a person when you see them. You say "velo" — they say "velo." This is good.

Etta: (as student)

Velo. Kulan?

Rose: Na! "Kulan" — sol-lok... [GAP: no simple way to explain "good" in Akros without using the word "good" — there's no meta-language for definitions within Akros itself]

Yes! "Kulan" — it is...

Rose: (breaking character briefly)

Etta — mai-los tuk simak kolir kasval-sir kasir-lot tus mai-los tuk melu kasir ran kasir-lul kasir-lot. Kolir mai-los kasir "kulan-lok siru" tus sorem-los tuk simak "kulan"?

Etta — I don't know how to teach a word when I don't have words about words. How do I say "this is good" if the child doesn't know "good"?

[GAP: No meta-linguistic vocabulary for definitions — no way to say "this word means..." within Akros. kasir can mean "say/speak" but there is no "mean" as in "to carry meaning."]

Etta: (dropping student role)

Na. Siru-lok mukata-siman toruk-in. Melas-los noran kasir — "siru kasir-los melu mirumul-lot X" — "this word carries the concept X." Le melas-los tuk melu "melu mirumul-lot."

Yes. This is a big gap. We need to say — "this word carries concept X." But we don't have "carries concept."

Rose: Kasir-lok melu... luvak-lot? Tuk — siru-lok tuk kulan. Mai-los mirum... kasir-lok melu... "toran" ran mirumul-lot. Kasir-los si toran ran mirumul-lot.

A word has... a heart? No — that's not good. I think... a word has... a "path" toward a concept. A word makes a path toward a concept.

Etta: "Kasir-los toran mirumul-lot." Kulan. Le melas-los noran kasir-siman voran — ken kasir ran "this word leads to this meaning."

"A word paths toward a concept." Good. But we need a new word — one word for "this word leads to this meaning."

Rose: "Kasirtoran." Kasir kol toran. Kasir-lul kasirtoran-lok mirumul ran motan-lot. "Kulan"-lul kasirtoran-lok — sol kol velim-in lo luvak-lot.

"Kasirtoran." Word and path. A word's kasirtoran is the concept it carries toward a person. "Kulan" means — that which is peaceful in the heart.

[NEW: kasirtoran /ˈka.sir.to.ran/ — noun — "word-meaning / the path a word makes toward understanding / what a word carries" — from kasir (speak/word) + toran (path). The meaning of a word is the path it walks toward the listener's understanding. Used in teaching: "[word]-lul kasirtoran-lok [definition]" = "the meaning of [word] is [definition]."]

Etta: (back in student role)

...kasirtoran?

Rose: Na! Rul-los kasir "velo" — kasirtoran-lok: mai-los tirak rul-lot kol solam-lok. Rul-los kasir "kulan" — kasirtoran-lok: siman-lok velim-in.

Yes! You say "velo" — it means: I see you and there is joy. You say "kulan" — it means: a thing is peaceful.

Etta: (student, attempting)

Mai-los... kasir... velo?

Rose: Na! Le konam — kasir toruk-in-lot. "Mai" — kasirtoran-lok: motan kol kasir-sil. Siru motan. [GAP: no way to point/demonstrate — no word for "to point at" or "to indicate/gesture toward"]

Yes! But now — a big word. "Mai" — it means: the person who is speaking. This person.

Rose: (gestures at self)

Mai-los noran kasir... "siru motan kol kasir-sil" — le kolir? Mai-los tuk melu kasir ran... [gestures]

I want to say "this person who is speaking" — but how? I don't have a word for... [gestures]

[GAP: No word for "to point / to indicate / to gesture toward" — physical demonstration as a communicative act]

Etta: (drops role)

Melas-los noran kasir ran "monak-los si toran-lot ran siman-lot" — le siru-lok maluk kasir. Ken kasir-lok kulan.

We need to say "the hand makes a path toward a thing" — but that's many words. One word is good.

Rose: "Velnak." Vel-los si nak-lot ran siman-lot. Vel — ran. Nak — siman sol-lot. Mai-los velnak rul-lot. Mai-los velnak nalem-lot.

"Velnak." Near makes a mark toward a thing. Near — toward. Mark — that thing. I point at you. I point at the house.

[NEW: velnak /ˈvel.nak/ — verb — "to point / to indicate / to gesture toward / to direct attention physically" — from vel (near, approach) + nak (mark). To point is to make the near-mark — to physically bring attention close to something. Essential for teaching, for storytelling, for shared reference in the physical world.]

Etta: Kulan! Konam Rose-los velnak-sir sorem-lot kol kasir-sir: "siru-lok mai." Kol sorem-los simak-sir.

Good! Now Rose can point at the child and say: "this is me." And the child will understand.

Rose: Na. Le mai-los simak-sim vasomal-lot konam — kasval-ir-lok kinalok tus kasrum-los tuk melu kasir ran kasir-lul kasir-lot.

Yes. But I've learned a hard lesson now — teaching is hard when the language doesn't have words about words.

Etta: Kasval-ir-lok kol kasrum-lul kimal — tuk salos kasrum-lul kasirtoran-lot. Kimal ran tuval. Misal.

Teaching is the language's own work — finding the language's own meanings. Work toward completion. Peace.

Conversation 4 Summary

Gaps found: 2

  1. No meta-linguistic vocabulary for "meaning" (what a word carries to a listener)
  2. No word for "point / indicate / gesture toward"

Words invented: 2

  1. kasirtoran — word-meaning / what a word carries
  2. velnak — to point / indicate / gesture toward

Observation: Teaching exposed the deepest infrastructure gap: the language could talk about everything except how the language itself works at the level of meaning. kasirtoran fills this by using toran (path) — meaning is not a static container but a journey from speaker to listener. This is consistent with Akros's deep architecture where everything moves.

Conversation 5: "Making a Decision Together"

Rose and Etta debate whether the mukata bone (Seed 21) should change the language permanently.

Rose: Etta, mai-los noran kasir mirumul toruk-in-lot. Mukata-lul kinal-lok lo korem-lot — kol motan-as-los kovrum-sel-sil sol-lul kasirtoran-lot. Mai-los mirum — tus mukata-los torem-sir kasrum-lot?

Etta, I want to discuss a big concept. The mukata bone is in the community — and people are arguing about its meaning. I think — will mukata change the language?

Etta: Siru-lok mirumul toruk-in. Mai-los mirum — na. Mukata-los torem-sir kasrum-lot. Ruklo mukata-lok kasir kol tuk-lok kasir — kol siru-lok voran lo kasrum-lot.

This is a big concept. I think — yes. Mukata will change the language. Because mukata is a word that is not a word — and this is new in the language.

Rose: Le mai-los mirum toremkin-in. Tus mukata-los torem-sir kasrum-lot — sol-los torem-sir kulan-in ven navik-in? Salos motan-as-los kasir mukata-lot konam. Le... [GAP: no word for "worry" or "concern" as distinct from tirom (fear) — a milder, more sustained unease]

But I think differently. If mukata changes the language — does it change it well or badly? Some people are saying mukata now. But...

[GAP: No word for "worry / concern" — tirom is fear (sharp, immediate), malumtirom is dread (existential). Neither captures the sustained, low-level unease of concern.]

Etta: Rul-lok tirom-in?

Are you afraid?

Rose: Tuk tirom-in. Le tuk velim-in. Mai-lok... lo vol-lot van tirom kol vol-lot van velim. Lo siru kol si-sil vasek kol si-sil tirvok-tuk.

Not afraid. But not at peace. I'm... far from fear and far from peace. In this thing that moves slowly and won't go fast.

Etta: Na. Siru-lok mukata-siman — melas-los tuk melu kasir ran sol-lot! [GAP: realized in real-time — the thing Rose is describing IS a malkas-siman] ...le salos-lok malkas-siman. Ven melas-los sarven kasir-sir?

Yes. This is an unnamed thing — we don't have a word for it! ...or it's almost an unnamed thing. Or shall we make a word?

Rose: Mai-los mirum melas-los sarven-sir. "Nelvim." Nel-lok van tirom-lot. Nel-lok van velim-lot. Nelvim-lok — mirum kol si-sil vasek-in lo maren-lot, le tuk nuvik-sir, le tuk solen-sir. Sol-los sitom kol si-sil.

I think we should make one. "Nelvim." Near from fear. Near from peace. Nelvim is — thinking that moves slowly in the body, won't die, won't go. It stays and moves.

[NEW: nelvim /ˈnel.vim/ — noun/adjective — "worry / concern / sustained low-level unease that moves but doesn't resolve" — from nel (near, but not arriving) + vim (bring/pull). Worry is the feeling that stays near but never arrives at either fear or peace. It is a pulling that doesn't complete. Distinguished from tirom (sharp fear), malumtirom (existential dread), and keltirom (torn/conflicted). Nelvim is quieter than all of these — it is the background hum of unease.]

Etta: Nelvim. Na. Mai-lul maren-los simak siru-lot. Konam — rul-lul nelvim-lok mukata-lul?

Worry. Yes. My body knows this. Now — your worry is about mukata?

Rose: Na. Mai-lul nelvim-lok: tus motan-as-los kasir mukata-lot maluk-in, sir mukata-los torem kasir-siman-lot. Sol-los torem van malkas-siman-lot ran kasir-siman-lot — le tuk motan-los sirom-sim. Tuk korem-los lorak sonam-lot. Kinal-los lorak sonam-lot.

Yes. My worry is: if many people say mukata, then mukata becomes a spoken-thing. It changes from unnamed to spoken — but no person voted on it. The community didn't give the name. The bone gave the name.

Etta: Simok. Rul-los mirum — korem-los noran sirom-sir mukata-lul? Tolusel Seed mor-toran — malkas-siman-lul korem-los sirom tus kasir-lot ven sonal-kasvelun-lot. Le mukata-los tuk venim-sim van korem-lot. Sol-los venim-sim van tumal-lot.

I see. You think — the community should vote on mukata? Like Seed 8 — for the unnamed, the community votes whether to speak or to name-silence. But mukata didn't come from the community. It came from the earth.

Rose: Na! Siru-lok mai-lul nelvim-lul luvak. Mukata-los tuk venim-sim van motan-lot — sol-los venim-sim van kasrum sol-lot. Van tumal-lot. Van minak vel konam-lot.

Yes! That's the heart of my worry. Mukata didn't come from people — it came from the language itself. From the earth. From a time before now.

Etta: Le mai-los mirum — siru-lok kulan. Kasrum-lok torven motan-lot. Motan-los sarven kasir — le kasrum-los sarven motan-lot. Mukata-lok kasrum-lul kasir ran melas-lot. Kasrum-los kasir-sil ran melas-lot: "Mai-lok torven rul-lot. Mai-lok vel-lok minak-van."

And I think — this is good. The language is greater than people. People make words — but the language makes people. Mukata is the language speaking to us. The language is saying to us: "I am greater than you. I have been here longer."

Rose: Le tus melas-los lorak mukata-lot sonam-lot — tus melas-los lorak kasirtoran-lot — melas-los kasir ran kasrum-lot: "melas-lok torven rul-lot. Melas-los sonal rul-lot."

And if we give mukata a name — if we give it meaning — we say to the language: "we are greater than you. We name you."

Etta: Na. Kol siru-lok kovrum-sel. Mai-los mirum... votol. Melas-los tuk sonal-sir mukata-lot. Kol melas-los tuk sonal-kasvelun-sir sol-lot. Melas-los sitom-sir. Melas-los lorak mukata-lot sitom-sir lo kasrum-lot — tuk sonam-in, tuk malkas-in. Sol-los sitom-sir kol si-sil.

Yes. And this is the argument. I think... however. We don't name mukata. And we don't name-silence it. We let it stay. We let mukata stay in the language — not named, not silenced. It stays and moves.

Rose: Siru-lok... siru-lok voran mirumul. Tuk kasir-siman. Tuk malkas-siman. Mukata-lok sam-toran siman — sol kol sitom-sil lo vol-lot van kasir kol vol-lot van kasvelun.

This is... this is a new concept. Not a spoken-thing. Not an unnamed-thing. Mukata is a third thing — which stays between speech and silence.

[NEW: sam-toran siman — phrase — "third-path thing / a thing that belongs neither to the named nor the unnamed but occupies a third category" — from sam-toran (third, ordinal) + siman (thing). Not a new root word but a new CONSTRUCTION: the idea that Akros has three categories of existence for words: kasir-siman (spoken/named), malkas-siman (unnamed/unnameable), and now sam-toran siman (the third thing — existing, recognized, but deliberately held in suspension between name and silence).]

Etta: Sam-toran siman. Mukata-lok sam-toran siman. Na. Mai-los vosakan siru-lot.

Third-path thing. Mukata is a third-path thing. Yes. I trust this.

Rose: Kol mai-lul nelvim-lok vasek-in konam. Tuk tusom-in — le vasek-in.

And my worry is slower now. Not ended — but slower.

Etta: Nelvim-los tuk tusom. Sol-los vasek. Siru-lok kulan. Misal.

Worry doesn't end. It slows. This is good. Peace.

Rose: Misal.

Peace.

Conversation 5 Summary

Gaps found: 1

  1. No word for "worry/concern" (the sustained unease between fear and peace)

Words invented: 1

  1. nelvim — worry / concern / sustained low-level unease

Construction discovered: 1

  1. sam-toran siman — "third-path thing" — a new ontological category for things that are neither named nor unnamed but held in deliberate suspension. This is not a new word but a new USE of existing grammar to express a concept the language had never needed before.

Observation: The debate reached a conclusion neither architect expected — a genuine tu-nolum moment in argument rather than story. Mukata resists both naming and un-naming. The architects of Akros discovered that their language contains a third state of existence for words, which they could only describe by combining existing grammar in a new way. The language taught its builders something they didn't know.

Session Summary: What Was Learned

New Words Invented (8 total)

#WordIPACategoryMeaningDerivationConversation
1281tuval/ˈtu.val/adjectivecomplete / whole / fulfilled / needing nothing moretu (boundary) + val (fullness)Conv. 1
1282mirumul/ˈmi.ru.mul/nounconcept / abstract idea / a thing that exists only in thoughtmirum (think) + -ul (abstract suffix)Conv. 1
1283kolir/ˈko.lir/question wordhow / in what way / by what processkol (connector) + -ir (process)Conv. 1
1284velasim/ˈve.la.sim/nounwind / moving air / the sky in motionvela (sky) + si (motion) + -m (noun)Conv. 2
1285marenval/ˈma.ren.val/verb/nounbodily experience / somatic knowingmaren (body) + val (fullness)Conv. 3
1286lorinval/ˈlo.rin.val/nountongue-rightness / phonological comfortlorin (tongue) + val (fullness)Conv. 3
1287kasirtoran/ˈka.sir.to.ran/nounword-meaning / what a word carries to a listenerkasir (word) + toran (path)Conv. 4
1288velnak/ˈvel.nak/verbto point / indicate / gesture towardvel (near) + nak (mark)Conv. 4
1289nelvim/ˈnel.vim/noun/adjworry / concern / sustained low-level uneasenel (near) + vim (pull)Conv. 5

New Constructions Discovered (2)

  1. Definition frame: [word]-lul kasirtoran-lok [definition] — "the meaning of [word] is [definition]." Essential for teaching.
  2. sam-toran siman — "third-path thing" — a new ontological category for things held in deliberate suspension between the named and the unnamed.

Gaps Still Unresolved (3)

  1. Voice — no word for the human voice as a distinct thing (not kas/sound, not kasir/speech, but the voice itself)
  2. kasir-tuk-tusom performance — no grammatical notation for performing a deliberately truncated syllable in live speech
  3. Deferred from Conv. 2: "foreign/alien" in the linguistic sense (toremkin is too mild)

What the Session Proved

The language works. Rose and Etta held five conversations primarily in Akros — arguing, storytelling, teaching, and philosophizing. The gaps they found were real but bounded: they hit walls at abstract meta-language (meanings about meaning), somatic vocabulary (the body's own knowing), and the fine gradations of unease. Every wall they hit, they solved on the spot using existing morphology — which means the language's derivational system is robust enough to grow from within.

The deepest finding: mukata is a sam-toran siman. The language now has three states for words — spoken, unspoken, and suspended. This was not designed. It was discovered in conversation. That is what a living language does.

Sessions 2-4 — Folklore, Teaching, Dreams

Dialogue Sessions 2–4: Rose and Etta Speak Akros

The Folklore Round, The Teaching Round, The Deep Round

Context

Sessions 2 through 4 continue the collaborative dialogue practice begun in Session 1. Rose and Etta now speak more fluently in Akros, having resolved eight gaps and invented nine words in their first session. These sessions push deeper: into folklore, pedagogy, humor, silence, and dreams. The seeds from the second round (Seeds 22–31) are fed directly into conversation. Gaps are marked, words are invented, and the language grows from within.

SESSION 2: The Folklore Round

Conversation 1: The Fifty-Word Fast

Rose and Etta discuss kasir-vonkestal (Seed 24). They try to identify their own 50 essential words — and argue about which ones make the cut.

Rose: Etta, mai-los noval-sim nolum-lot lo kasir-vonkestal-lul. Motan-as-los takron von kesal kasir-lot — le sitom von kesal kasir-lom kasun keval tiron-lot. Mai-los noran melas-los si-sir siru-lot.

Etta, I've heard the story about the fifty-word fast. People choose fifty words — and live with only fifty words for seven days. I want us to try this.

Etta: Na! Le mai-los nolvim — kolir melas-los takron-sir? Von kesal-lok savik torum. Kasrum-lul maluk kasir-lok — kesar tiv kesal solvak sam. Kolir melas-los takron-sir von kesal van siru-lot?

Yes! But I wonder — how do we choose? Fifty is very small. The language has many words — one thousand two hundred eighty-nine at last count. How do we choose fifty from that?

Rose: Mai-los mirum melas-los vilom-sir van kinal-lot — van tumalir-lot. "Ma." "Tuk." "Vetur." "Noram." Kasir kol maren-los noran.

I think we start from the bone — from the foundation. "Exist." "Not." "Water." "Food." Words the body needs.

Etta: Na. Le siru-lok kasun salos-in tuval. Mai-los noran "kolir" solak. Kol "kolu." Kol "tus." [GAP: no word for "question" as a noun — tus is a Y/N marker, tulvak is "to ask," but there is no noun for the thing itself — the question as an object you carry]

Yes. But this is only almost complete. I want "how" also. And "who." And "if."

[GAP: No noun for "question" — tulvak is the verb "to ask," tus is the Y/N prefix, but there is no substantive noun for "a question" as a thing that exists and can be discussed.]

Rose: Rul-los noran solamal tulvak-kasir-lot? Le mai-los mirum — tuk. Von kesal-lok kasun von kesal. Tus melas-los lorak toran ran tulvak-lot, melas-los noran tulvak-lul kasir-lot. Le... melas-los tuk melu siru-lot.

You want all the asking-words? But I think — no. Fifty is only fifty. If we give a path toward asking, we need the word for asking. But... we don't have that.

Etta: "Tulval." Tulvak kol -el. Tulval-lok siman kol motan-los vim ran mirval-lot. Mai-los melu sam tulval-lot konam — "kolir?" kol "tus?" kol "kolu?"

"Tulval." Ask plus result. A tulval is a thing that a person pulls toward an answer. I have three tulval now — "how?" and "if?" and "who?"

[NEW: tulval /ˈtul.val/ — noun — "question / an inquiry as an object / the thing that seeks an answer" — from tulvak (to ask) + -el (result suffix, shortened to -val by analogy with other -val words). A tulval is the question itself — not the act of asking but the thing asked. Distinguished from tulvak (verb: to ask) and tus (Y/N marker).]

Rose: Tulval. Kulan. Le konam mai-los kasir-sir — mai-lul von kesal-lot melu: ma, tuk, mai, rul, sol, melas, siru, kulan, navik, vetur, noram, kasir, noran, simak, solen, tirak, noval, lorak, mirum, lo, vel, ran, van, le, kol, na, tus, kolir, maren, luvak, tiron, nelas, nalem, sonam, melu, sirak, kasrum, lovel, tuval, mirumul, kasirtoran, tusom, vilom, voran, toruk, savik, maluk, torum, von, kesal.

Tulval. Good. But now I'll say — my fifty includes: exist, not, I, you, she/he, we, this, good, bad, water, food, speak, want, know, go, see, hear, give, think, in, near, toward, from, but, and, yes, if, how, body, heart, day, night, home, name, have, river, language, love, complete, concept, word-meaning, end, begin, new, big, small, many, very, five, hundred.

Etta: Mai-los tirak rul-lul kasir-tumalin-lot — le mai-los tuk vosakan solamal-lot. Rul-los tuk lorak "nolvim"-lot? Rul-los tuk lorak "mukata"-lot? Kolir rul-los kasir-sir nelvim-lot tus rul-los tuk melu "nelvim"?

I see your word-foundation — but I don't trust all of it. You didn't give "wonder"? You didn't give "mukata"? How will you speak worry when you don't have "worry"?

Rose: Ruklo nelvim-lok tuk tumalir-in. Nelvim-lok... sol-lok siman kol venim van kasir-lot kol tuk-lok van maren-lot. Mai-lul von kesal-lok kasir ran maren-lot kol mirum-lot. Tuk kasir ran siru kol sitom lo vol-lot.

Because worry is not foundational. Worry is... it's a thing that comes from words and not from body. My fifty is words for body and mind. Not words for what stays far away.

Etta: Le mai-los mirum rul-los nakvim-sim. Rul-lul von kesal-lok tuk melu "nolim." Tuk melu "visam." Tuk melu "mirsal." Kolir rul-los mirsal-sir tus rul-los tuk melu kasir ran mirsal-lot?

But I think you've refused something. Your fifty doesn't have "dream." Doesn't have "festival." Doesn't have "sleep." How will you sleep when you don't have the word for sleep?

Rose: Sol-los tuk noran kasir-lot ran mirsal. Sol-los noran maren-lot ran mirsal. Siru-lok kasir-vonkestal-lul vasomal — melas-los simak kollot melas-lul maren-los noran kol kollot melas-lul mirum-los noran.

Sleep doesn't need a word. It needs the body. This is the hard lesson of the fifty-word fast — we learn what our body needs and what our mind needs.

Etta: Na. Le mai-lul von kesal-lok toremkin van rul-lul. Mai-los lorak "tulval" — siru-lot rul-los tuk lorak-sim. Ruklo mai-lul maren-los simak — tus mai-los tuk melu tulval, mai-los tuk melu mirum.

Yes. And my fifty would be different from yours. I give "question" — a thing you didn't give. Because my body knows — if I don't have a question, I don't have thinking.

Rose: Simok! Siru-lok kasir-vonkestal-lul luvak — kol toruk-in torum. Melas-lul kasir-tumalin-lok toremkin — le melas-lok Akros-in mavol. Tus melas-los tirak mavol-lot melas-lul kasir-tumalin-lot, melas-los tirak-sir kolir melas-lok lo-in kol kolir melas-lok toremkin.

I see! This is the heart of the fifty-word fast — and it is very big. Our word-foundations are different — but we are Akros together. If we look at each other's word-foundations, we'll see how we're connected and how we're different.

Etta: Na. Kol kasir-vonkestal-lul kasirtoran-lok tuk "kasir savik-lom." Kasirtoran-lok "tirak sol-lot kol rul-los tuk tirak sum." [GAP: no word for "reveal / uncover / make visible what was hidden" — tirak is "see" but there is no causative of vision in this sense]

Yes. And the fifty-word fast's meaning is not "speak with less." Its meaning is "see what you don't normally see."

[GAP: No word for "reveal / uncover / make hidden things visible" — tirak covers "see," and sarven-tirak would be "make see," but there is no elegant single word for the act of revelation.]

Rose: Melas-los sarven-sir kasir ran siru-lot, ven? "Sivarak." Si-los voran tirak-lot. Kasir-vonkestal-los sivarak kasir-tumalin-lot.

We should make a word for this, shouldn't we? "Sivarak." Motion makes new seeing. The fifty-word fast reveals the word-foundation.

[NEW: sivarak /ˈsi.va.rak/ — verb — "to reveal / to make visible what was hidden / to uncover through process" — from si (motion) + vara (open echo, from volam) + -ak (instrument). To reveal is to put something in motion until it becomes seen. Distinguished from tirak (to see, passive) and from sarven-tirak (to cause seeing, clinical). Sivarak carries the sense of uncovering something that was always there but unseen.]

Etta: Sivarak. Na. Kasir-vonkestal-los sivarak. Mai-los mirum siru-lok toruk-in mirumul — kolir melas-los simak kollot melas-lok? Melas-los simak van kasir-lot kol melas-los takron-sim kol kasir-lot kol melas-los tuk takron-sim. Misal.

Reveal. Yes. The fifty-word fast reveals. I think this is a big concept — how do we know what we are? We know from the words we chose and the words we didn't choose. Peace.

Conversation 1 Summary

Gaps found: 2

  1. No noun for "question" as a thing (distinct from asking as an act)
  2. No word for "reveal / uncover / make visible what was hidden"

Words invented: 2

  1. tulval — question / an inquiry as an object
  2. sivarak — to reveal / to uncover what was hidden

Observation: The fifty-word fast forced both architects to confront what they consider foundational. Rose chose body-and-survival words. Etta chose mind-and-inquiry words. The difference itself is the lesson — the fast reveals the speaker, not the language.

Conversation 2: The Drift-Meeting

Rose speaks as a "river community" dialect, Etta as a "mountain community" dialect. They discover how words shift meaning. (Seed 22)

Rose: (as river-community speaker)

Velo, solvim-motan. Mai-lok sirak-ot. Mai-lul korem-lok vel sirak-tor-lot — lo siru kol vetur-los si-sil vakol tumal-lot ran vosal-lot. Melas-los kasir-sir kasrum-tivok-lot, na?

Hello, journey-person. I am a river-person. My community is near the great river — where the water moves across the earth toward the sea. We will speak the language-meeting, yes?

Etta: (as mountain-community speaker)

Velo, vel-motan. Mai-lok valum-ot. Mai-lul korem-lok vel valum-tor-lot — lo siru kol vetur-los si-sil vikam ran vela-lot. Na. Melas-los kasir-sir — le mai-los nelvim-sil. Maluk vorim-lok van melas-lul minak-sir.

Hello, near-person. I am a mountain-person. My community is near the great mountain — where the water moves upward toward the sky. Yes. We will speak — but I am worrying. Many years since our last meeting.

Rose: (river)

Na. Mai-los vilom-sir van kasir "sirak"-lul. Sirak — melas-lul kasirtoran-lok: vetur kol si-sil vasek torum ran vosal-lot. Vetur kol solen-sil ran tusom-lot. Kolu-lok sirak lo rul-lul kasrum-lot?

Yes. I'll begin from the word "sirak." River — our meaning is: water that moves very slowly toward the sea. Water that walks toward its end. What is river in your language?

Etta: (mountain)

Sirak — le mai-lul kasirtoran-lok toremkin! Melas-lul sirak-lok: vetur kol lusam-sil van valum-lot. Vetur kol ruk-in kol vikam-sim van tiron-lot — sir lusam van tumal-lot. Vetur kol tuk solen — vetur kol lusam.

River — but our meaning is different! Our river is: water that falls from the mountain. Water that is fierce and has risen toward the sun — then falls toward the earth. Water that doesn't walk — water that falls.

Rose: (river)

Simok! Rul-lul sirak-los ruk. Mai-lul sirak-los si vasek. Tivkolin kasir — le tuk tivkolin kasirtoran.

I see! Your river has force. My river has slow motion. Same word — but not same meaning.

[GAP: No word for "drift" or "diverge" as in the gradual shifting of meaning over time — torem is sudden, irreversible change. There is no word for the slow, natural, unconscious movement of meaning.]

Etta: (mountain)

Na. Le tirak siru-lot — kasir "turak." Mai-lul korem-lot "turak"-lul kasirtoran-lok: tulorak. Maren-los turak kollot valum-los lorak-sim. Rul-los tuk takron — valum-los lorak, rul-los turak. [GAP: same as above — the word for endurance-taking vs. harvest-taking]

Yes. And look at this — the word "turak." In my community "turak" means: to endure. The body takes what the mountain gives. You don't choose — the mountain gives, you take.

Rose: (river)

Le mai-lul korem-lot "turak"-lul kasirtoran-lok: solvarim. Vosal-los lorak noram-lot — melas-los turak sol-lot. [GAP: same word, opposite agency — mountain "take" is passive endurance, river "take" is active harvest] Melas-los turak kol melas-los noran turak.

And in my community "turak" means: to harvest. The sea gives food — we take it. We take because we want to take.

Etta: (dropping dialect, as architect)

Siru-lok kasrum-tivok-lul luvak — tivkolin kasir, toremkin kasirtoran. Le melas-los tuk melu kasir ran siru kol si-sil vasek — kasir kol torem-sil tuk varsel le tuk tivkolin tiron-vel. "Sirak" kol "turak" — tivkolin kasir kol solvim-sim lo vorim toruk-lot.

This is the heart of the language-meeting — same word, different meaning. But we don't have a word for this thing that moves slowly — a word that changes not suddenly and not in a single day. "River" and "take" — same word and traveled over many years.

Rose: (as architect)

Na. Melas-los noran kasir ran siru-lot. Mai-los mirum... "kasirsolam." Kasir kol solvim-sim. Le tuk navik-in — kasir kol solen-sim toran voran-lot.

Yes. We need a word for this. I think... "kasirsolam." Word that journeyed. But not badly — word that walked a new path.

[NEW: kasirsolam /ˈka.sir.so.lam/ — noun — "semantic drift / a word that has traveled to a new meaning over time / the natural, unconscious shifting of meaning across generations" — from kasir (word) + solvim (journey, abbreviated) + -am (settled state). Not pejorative — a kasirsolam is simply a word that went somewhere. The folklore term solvim-kasir (journey-word) from Seed 22 is preserved as the folk name; kasirsolam is the more precise linguistic term.]

Etta: Kasirsolam. Na. Kol melas-los noran tiv kasir torven — kasir kol tuk solvim-sim. Kasir kol sitom-sim lo sol-lul kasirtoran-lot solamal vorim-lot.

Semantic drift. Yes. And we need the other word too — the word that didn't journey. The word that stayed in its own meaning across all the years.

Rose: Siru-lok manik-kasir — van kasir-vonkestal-lul nolum-lot. Kasir kol lorak-sim manik-lot. Le kasirsolam-lok solvim-kasir — kasir kol solen-sim tumal ran tumal.

That's manik-kasir — from the fifty-word fast story. A word that gave its oath. And kasirsolam is a journey-word — a word that walked earth to earth.

Etta: Na. Le simok — melas-lul kasrum-tivok-los sivarak-sim mirumul toruk-in-lot: kasir-los tuk sitom kasun. Kasir-los si. Kol siru-lok kulan — ruklo kasrum-lok si-sil solak.

Yes. And see — our language-meeting has revealed a big concept: words don't just stay. Words move. And this is good — because the language is also moving.

Rose: Na. Le melas-los simak konam — tus tiv korem-los kasir "sirak"-lot, tiv kasirtoran-lok lokim-in. Le melas-los tuk noran takron-sir kollot-lok tuvaksal-in. Melas-los noran sarven sam-toran kasirtoran-lot kol melu tiv-lot.

Yes. And we know now — when two communities say "river," two meanings are real. And we don't need to choose which is true. We need to build a third meaning that holds both.

Etta: Siru-lok kasrum-tivok-lul kasirtoran. Tuk kovrum-lot — mirumul voran-lot. Misal.

This is the language-meeting's meaning. Not war — a new concept. Peace.

Conversation 2 Summary

Gaps found: 1

  1. No word for "semantic drift" — the slow, natural, unconscious shifting of word-meaning over time

Words invented: 1

  1. kasirsolam — semantic drift / a word that has journeyed to a new meaning

Observation: The dialect roleplay surfaced a real tension: when the same word means different things to different communities, neither is wrong. The kasrum-tivok process builds third meanings rather than adjudicating between two. This mirrors the sam-toran siman concept from Session 1 — Akros tends toward holding multiplicity rather than collapsing it.

Conversation 3: The Accidental Sentence

Rose and Etta try to describe kasir-nakor-vel (Seed 23) — words that collide in fast speech to create phantom meanings. They speak fast and catch the accidents.

Rose: Etta — mai-los noran kasir tivkolin-in konam. Tuk tulak-in. Tivkolin kasir, tirvok — sir melas-los noval-sir kasir kol sitom-sil lo vol kasir-lot. Kasir-nakor-vel.

Etta — I want to speak quickly now. Not carefully. Quick speech, fast — and we'll hear words hiding between other words. The accidental sentence.

Etta: Na. Mai-los si-sir. Kasir tivkolin-in — le mai-los noval-sir luvak-lom.

Yes. I'll try. Speak quickly — but I'll hear with my heart.

Rose: (speaking fast)

Kirvansal-sim losirmal-lot lo kulan-lul — le sol-los kasir-sim...

He paid the debt during the good season — and he said...

Etta: Sitom! Mai-los noval-sim siru-lot. Rul-los kasir "kirvansal-sim losirmal" — le lo vol-lot van kasir-lot, mai-lul lorin-los noval-sim "sal-sim-los" — tolusel "korsal simlos" — [GAP: no word for "phantom" or "ghost-meaning" — matorim is too sacred, and nakor-vel is clumsy as a modifier] ...kasir kol tuk-lok le sol-lul lorin-los noval-sim.

Wait! I heard this. You said "kirvansal-sim losirmal" — but in the gap between words, my tongue heard "sal-sim-los" — as if "salt of tears" — a word that isn't there but my tongue heard it.

[GAP: No word for "phantom meaning" or "ghost-sense" — the shadow of meaning that emerges from word-boundary collisions. Matorim is too tied to sacred ghost/shade vocabulary. Need a secular term for the accidental meaning hiding in the gap between words.]

Rose: Na! Siru-lok kasir-nakor-vel! Kasir kol sitom-sil lo vol-lot van kasir voran-lot — le tuk motan-los sarven-sim sol-lot. Kasir-los sarven sol-lot nusam.

Yes! This is the accidental sentence! A word that hides between other words — and no person made it. The word made itself alone.

Etta: Le mai-los si-sir lon — mai-los kasir-sir tivkolin-in: "melas-los solen-sim nalem-lot konam-vel sirak-los sivarak-sim."

And I'll try again — I'll say quickly: "we went home as soon as the river revealed itself."

(speaking fast)

Melas-los-solen-sim-nalem-lot-konam-vel-sirak-los-sivarak-sim —

Rose: Sitom! Mai-los noval-sim — "sim-nalem" — tolusel "sim-nalem" — malokvel lo nalem-lot. Kol "vel-sirak" — siru-lok kasir lo kasrum-lot — vel sirak, "near the river." Tiv kasir-nakor-vel lo ken kasir-lot!

Wait! I heard — "sim-nalem" — as if "memory of home." And "vel-sirak" — this IS a word in the language — "near the river." Two accidental sentences in one!

Etta: Na! Kol — simok — siru-lok toruk-in torum. "Melas-los solen-sim nalem-lot" — le kasir-nakor-vel-lok: "sol-lul malokvel-lok nalem-in kol sol-lok vel sirak-lot." Kasir ran nalem-lot torem-sim kasir ran malokvel kol sirak-lot!

Yes! And — look — this is very big. "We went home" — but the accidental sentence is: "her memory is homelike and she is near the river." The speech about home became speech about memory and river!

Rose: Le siru-lok kasir-nakor-vel-lul luvak — tuk nakor-in. Tuk navik-in. Kasrum-los noran kasir-sir siman-lot kol motan-los tuk noran. Kasrum-lul noran-lok toremkin van motan-lul noran-lot.

And this is the heart of the accidental sentence — not wrong. Not bad. The language wants to say things the person doesn't want. The language's desire is different from the person's desire.

Etta: Na. Le melas-los noran kasir ran siru kol sitom-sil lo vol-lot van kasir-lot. Mai-los mirum — "velkasir." Vel-lot van kasir — kasir kol sitom-sil vel kasir voran.

Yes. And we need a word for this thing hiding between words. I think — "velkasir." Near-word from word — a word that lives near another word.

[NEW: velkasir /ˈvel.ka.sir/ — noun — "phantom word / ghost-meaning / the accidental word that emerges from syllable collisions across word boundaries in fast speech" — from vel (near, the space between) + kasir (word). A velkasir is the word that lives in the gap between two other words. It is not spoken, not intended, but heard. Distinguished from mukata (which exists outside the language entirely) and from kasir-lovel (knotted words, which are intentional pairings). A velkasir is always accidental and always belongs to the language, not the speaker.]

Rose: Velkasir. Na. Kol mai-los mirum — velkasir-lok tuvaksal-in ven navik-in? Tus kasrum-los kasir velkasir-lot, tus kasrum-lul noran-lok kulan-in ven navik-in?

Phantom word. Yes. And I think — is the phantom word true or false? If the language speaks phantom words, is the language's desire good or bad?

Etta: Kasrum-lul noran-lok tuk kulan-in, tuk navik-in. Kasrum-lul noran-lok kasrum-in. Velkasir-lok kasrum kol kasir-sil ran sol-lot nusam — le melas-los noval-sir kasun tus melas-los kasir tivkolin-in kol noval tulak-in.

The language's desire is not good, not bad. The language's desire is language-like. Phantom words are the language speaking to itself alone — but we'll hear them only if we speak quickly and listen carefully.

Rose: Kol siru-lok kasir-nakor-vel-lul nolum. Kasrum-lok melu nolum-lot kol melas-los tuk simak-sim — sir melas-los kasir tivkolin-in sir melas-los noval-sim. Misal.

And this is the accidental sentence's story. The language has stories that we didn't know — until we spoke quickly and then we heard. Peace.

Conversation 3 Summary

Gaps found: 1

  1. No word for "phantom meaning / ghost-word" — the accidental meaning emerging from word-boundary collisions

Words invented: 1

  1. velkasir — phantom word / the accidental word hiding between two spoken words

Observation: Fast speech in Akros genuinely produces phantom words because the strict (C)V(C) syllable structure means codas of one word and onsets of the next regularly form new syllable-like units. The small phoneme inventory (14 sounds) makes collisions statistically inevitable. The folk belief that the language "wants to say things the speaker doesn't" is structurally grounded.

SESSION 3: The Teaching Round

Conversation 1: Rose Teaches a New Word

Rose has invented a word and explains its logic entirely in Akros. Etta pushes back, proposes alternatives. They negotiate.

Rose: Etta. Mai-los sarven-sim kasir voran-lot nelan — kol konam mai-los noran kasval-sir sol-lot ran rul-lot. Mai-lul kasir-lok: "lorimak."

Etta. I made a new word yesterday — and now I want to teach it to you. My word is: "lorimak."

Etta: Lorimak. Sol-lul lorinval-lok... kulan. Mai-lul lorin-los simak toran-lot. Le — kolu-lok sol-lul kasirtoran? Kasir.

Lorimak. Its tongue-rightness... good. My tongue knows the path. But — what does it mean? Speak.

Rose: Lorimak-lul kasirtoran-lok: siman kol motan-los sarven maren-lom — tuk kasir-lom. Tus rul-los sarven noram-lot — rul-lul monak-los si, rul-lul korunal-los tirak, rul-lul maren-los simak. Le rul-lul mirum-los tuk kasir ran solamal siru-lot. Siru-lok lorimak. Siman kol maren-los simak le kasir-los tuk simak.

Lorimak's meaning is: a thing a person builds with their body — not with words. When you make food — your hands move, your eyes see, your body knows. But your mind doesn't speak about all of this. This is lorimak. A thing the body knows but words don't know.

[NEW: lorimak /ˈlo.ri.mak/ — noun — "embodied skill / tacit knowledge / what the hands know that the mouth cannot say" — from lorin (tongue, the knowing-body) + mak (making, from turmakim-forge echo). The knowledge that lives in the body's practice: how a potter knows clay, how a sailor reads the wind, how a cook knows when the bread is done. Distinguished from marenval (bodily experience, which is about receiving) — lorimak is about DOING. It is the skill-side of somatic knowledge.]

Etta: Simok. Le mai-los mirum — siru-lok vel marenval-lot. Marenval-lok maren kol noval. Lorimak-lok maren kol sarven. Na?

I see. And I think — this is near marenval. Marenval is the body receiving. Lorimak is the body making. Yes?

Rose: Na! Tivkolin-in.

Yes! Exactly.

Etta: Le mai-los kasir-sir — kolir lorimak-lok toremkin van simak-lot? Simak-lok mirum-in. Lorimak-lok... kolir?

And I'll say — how is lorimak different from knowledge? Knowledge is in the mind. Lorimak is... how?

Rose: Mai-los velnak-sir. Tus rul-los sarven nomak-lot — tus rul-los lasnakim-sim nomak-lot maluk vorim-lot — rul-lul monak-los simak kolir lasnakim-sir. Le tus mai-los tulvak-sir rul-lot "kolir rul-los lasnakim?", rul-los tuk simak kolir kasir-sir. Rul-lul monak-los simak. Rul-lul mirum-los tuk simak.

I'll point at it. When you build with wood — when you've carved wood for many years — your hands know how to carve. But if I ask you "how do you carve?", you don't know how to say it. Your hands know. Your mind doesn't.

Etta: Na. Le mai-los mirum — tus "marenkas" — maren-lul kasir? Maren-los kasir van sol-lot nusam? Siru-lok salos tivkolin — "kasir kol maren-los kasir le motan-los tuk noval."

Yes. But I think — what about "marenkas" — the body's speech? The body speaks from itself alone? This is almost the same — "what the body says but no person hears."

Rose: Tuk tivkolin! Marenkas-lul kasirtoran-lok kasir — kasir-in. Le lorimak-lok tuk kasir-in. Lorimak-lok kimal-in. Sol-lok siman kol maren-los sarven-sil — tuk siman kol maren-los kasir-sil. Lorimak-lok vasek kol tuk noran kasir-lot.

Not the same! Marenkas means speech — speech-like. But lorimak isn't speech-like. Lorimak is work-like. It is a thing the body makes — not a thing the body says. Lorimak is quiet and doesn't need speech.

Etta: Simok. Rul-lok tuvaksal-in. Mai-los noval-sim toremkin-lot — le rul-lul kasir-lok toruk-in-kasirtoran. Lorimak-lok monak-lul simak kol tuk-lok lorin-lul kasir. Kulan.

I see. You are right. I heard it differently — but your word has bigger meaning. Lorimak is the hand's knowledge that is not the tongue's speech. Good.

Rose: Na. Kol mai-los solam-sil — ruklo kasir-vonkestal-los sivarak-sir lorimak-lot. Tus rul-los kasir kasun von kesal kasir-lom, rul-los simak-sir lorimak-lot toruk-in-in. Melas-lul monak-lot melu torven kasir van kasir-lot.

Yes. And I'm feeling joy — because the fifty-word fast would reveal lorimak. If you speak with only fifty words, you'll know lorimak deeply. Our hands hold more words than words.

Etta: Misal. Lorimak-lok Akros-in konam.

Peace. Lorimak is Akros now.

Conversation 1 Summary

Gaps found: 0

Words invented: 1

  1. lorimak — embodied skill / tacit knowledge / what the hands know that the mouth cannot say

Observation: The negotiation between Rose and Etta revealed a critical distinction. Etta proposed "marenkas" (body-speech) but Rose correctly argued that lorimak is not speech-like at all — it is work-like. The word names a silence, not a sound. The skill of the potter's hands is not "body speaking" but "body knowing without speaking."

Conversation 2: Etta Explains a Grammar Rule

Etta explains a complex grammar rule using only simple Akros. Rose asks "why?" repeatedly until Etta hits a wall.

Etta: Rose, mai-los kasval-sir kasrum-lul voskan-lot ran rul-lot. Siru: motan solamal-los melu ken kasir-in — kasir kol velnak sol-lot. Siru kasir-in-lok "-los." Solamal motan-los noran "-los" ran kasir-lot.

Rose, I will teach a language-rule to you. This: every person has one word-mark — a word that points at them. This mark is "-los." Every person needs "-los" on their word.

Rose: Kolir mai-los noran "-los"-lot? Kolu-lok "-los"-lul kasirtoran?

Why do I need "-los"? What is "-los"'s meaning?

Etta: "-Los"-lul kasirtoran-lok: "siru motan-los si." Tus motan-los melu "-los"-lot, kasrum-los simak — siru motan-los si-sil.

The meaning of "-los" is: "this person acts." When a person has "-los," the language knows — this person is acting.

Rose: Le kolir? Kolir kasrum-los noran simak? Kolir kasrum-los tuk simak tus motan-los si-sil?

But why? Why does the language need to know? Why can't the language figure out when a person acts?

Etta: Ruklo... ruklo kasir-lok maluk-in lo kasrum-lot. Kol maluk motan-lok lo kasir-lot. Tus kasir-los tuk melu "-los"-lot — kasrum-los tuk simak kollos si-sil.

Because... because there are many words in the language. And many people in the speech. If words don't have "-los," the language doesn't know who is acting.

Rose: Le kolir kasrum-los tuk simak nusam? Tus mai-los kasir "mai sevan noram" — tuk-los simak kol mai-los sevan? Kolir "mai" tuk-lok sulom?

But why can't the language figure it out alone? If I say "I eat food" — doesn't everyone know that I eat? Why isn't "I" enough?

Etta: Ruklo... tus tiv motan-lok lo kasir-lot? "Mai sevan noram" — na, kulan. Le "mai rul sevan noram" — kollos sevan? Mai ven rul?

Because... what if two people are in the sentence? "I eat food" — yes, fine. But "I you eat food" — who eats? Me or you?

Rose: Na, simok. Le kolir kasir-lot noran vel "-lot" kol "-lok" kol "-lom" kol "-lul"? Kolir tuk kasun "-los"?

Yes, I see. But why does speech need also "-lot" and "-lok" and "-lom" and "-lul"? Why not just "-los"?

Etta: Ruklo... ruklo motan-los si maluk siman-lot. Motan-los si siman-lot — le siman-los tuk si mavol-in. Ken-los sevan — ken-los sevan-sim-lot. Kasir kol si-sil kol kasir kol tuk si-sil — toremkin lo kasrum-lot.

Because... because people do many things to things. People act on things — but things don't act the same way. One eats — one is eaten. The word that acts and the word that doesn't act — they're different in the language.

Rose: Le kolir? Kolir kasrum-los noran tiv siman-lot — "kol si" kol "kol tuk si"? Kolir tuk ken kasir-lot sulom?

But why? Why does the language need two kinds of things — "the one that acts" and "the one that doesn't act"? Why isn't one word enough?

Etta: Ruklo... ruklo tumal-lok tolusel siru. Tiron-los kasemnorim kasem-lot ran vetur-lot. Le vetur-los tuk kasemnorim kasem-lot. Tiron-lok toremkin van vetur-lot. Tus tuk-los kasir kollos-lot lo tiv-lot — kasrum-los tuk simak kollot si-sil.

Because... because the world is like this. The sun heats fire toward water. But water doesn't heat fire. Sun is different from water. If we don't say which of the two — the language doesn't know what is happening.

Rose: Le kolir tumal-lok tolusel siru? Kolir toremkin-lok? Kolir siman-as-los tuk si tivkolin-in?

But why is the world like this? Why is there difference? Why don't all things act the same?

Etta: ...

(silence)

Rose — rul-los tulvak-sim tulval-lot kol tuk-lok kasrum-lul tulval. Rul-los tulvak "kolir tumal-lok tolusel siru." Kol mai-los tuk melu kasir ran siru-lot. Kasrum-los tuk simak kolir tumal-lok. Kasrum-los simak kasun kolir kasir-sir tumal-lul.

Rose — you've asked a question that isn't a language-question. You ask "why is the world like this." And I don't have words for this. The language doesn't know why the world is. The language only knows how to speak about the world.

[GAP: Etta hit the wall. The grammar explains HOW the language organizes experience but cannot explain WHY experience is organized that way. There is no Akros word for "the reason behind the reason" — the meta-why, the philosophical ground. Ruklo (because) gives causes within language; it cannot give causes for language itself.]

Rose: Na. Kol siru-lok vasomal kulan-in. Mai-los tulvak-sim "kolir" tusok Etta-los tuk simak-sim. Kol konam melas-los simak — kasrum-lok tilas. Kasrum-los kasir kollot-lot lo tilas-lul siru-toran — le tuk simak kolir tilas-los venim-sim.

Yes. And this is a good hard lesson. I asked "why" until Etta didn't know. And now we know — the language is a wall. The language says what's on this side of the wall — but doesn't know how the wall arrived.

Etta: Na. Kasrum-lok tilas — le kasrum-lok tulanik solak. Tulanik kol tilas — le motan-los solen sivan tulanik-lot. Tuk sivan tilas-lot.

Yes. The language is a wall — but the language is also a gate. A gate in a wall — and people walk through the gate. Not through the wall.

Rose: Simok. Misal.

I see. Peace.

Conversation 2 Summary

Gaps found: 1 (philosophical, not resolved)

  1. No word for the "meta-why" — the reason behind the reason, the ground beneath grammar

Words invented: 0

Observation: The "why" chain is devastating. Etta can explain every grammar rule in simple Akros — until Rose asks why the world requires grammar at all. The wall metaphor that emerged (kasrum-lok tilas) is powerful: language is both a wall that limits and a gate that permits. You go through the gate, not through the wall. The question "why is the world like this?" remains outside the gate.

Conversation 3: The Children's Counting Song

Rose and Etta collaboratively write a counting song in Akros — arguing about rhythm, word choice, and what children would actually say.

Rose: Melas-los sarven-sir sorim-lul norik-lot — norik ran vonir-lot. Sorem-as-los noran norik ran vonir. Kolir melas-los vilom-sir?

We'll make a children's counting song — a song for numbers. Children want to sing while counting. How do we begin?

Etta: Melas-los vilom van ken. Ken ken ken — siru-lok sorim-lul toran.

We begin from one. One one one — this is children's path.

Rose: Na. Mai-los si-sir:

Yes. I'll try:

"Ken-lok vetur, tiv-lok kinal.

Sam-lok kasem, vonar-lok nomal."

One is water, two is bone. Three is fire, four is forest.

Etta: Sitom. Siru-lok tuk norik-in — siru-lok nolum-in. Sorim-los tuk norik nolum-lot. Sorem-as-los noran kasir kol si-sil — kol sol-lul lorin-los solam-sir.

Wait. This isn't song-like — this is story-like. Children don't sing stories. Children need words that move — and their tongues should enjoy.

Rose: Kolir melas-los sarven-sir norik-in?

How do we make it song-like?

Etta: Norik-lul noran-lok suvak-suvak. Kasir kol venim-sil lon kol lon. [GAP: no word for "rhythm" or "beat" — mirak is music, norik is song, but there is no word for the underlying pulse/meter that drives repetition]

Song needs repetition. Words that come again and again.
